docs.title: Natal
docs.text: create Natal Chart in SVG with ease

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• SVG natal chart generation in pure python
  • astrological entities in Enum to enhance DX
  • better code intellisense
  • more accurate copilot completion
  • enhanced type safety
  • high precision astrological data with Swiss Ephemeris
  • timezone, latitude and longitude database from GeoNames
  • docstrings with examples
  • test with doctest and pytest

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• installation

pip install natal

  • quick start
from natal import Astro, Chart

# create object for the natal chart data
natal_data = NatalData(
  name = "Person1",
  yr = 1980, mo = 1, day = 1,
  hr = 12, min = 0,
  city = "Taipei"
)

# return SVG string, optionally export to file
svg = Chart(natal_data, output="./natal-chart.svg")


## -- retrieve natal chart properties -- ##
# houses curp
natal_data.houses

# aspect(angle) between planets
natal_data.aspects

# percentage of planet in each element(fire, earth, air, water)
natal_data.element_percentage
  • sample output

refer the documentation for chart configuration and other details

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text: Creating a natal chart is the foundation of developing astrological software. Traditionally, the Python ecosystem has focused more on the "data" side, such as the calculation of astrological entities (planet positions, house cusps, etc.) but not as much on the "visualization" side, which involves plotting the natal chart in a graphical format. Current tools are outdated, not very Pythonic, and not friendly to code completion or Copilot(as they often present data in generic types eg. str, list, dict...). Not to mention the lack docstrings, examples, and proper documentation.

docs.title: Tech Stack
docs.text:
• Dominate for creating and manipulating XML(SVG)
  • pyswisseph python extension to the Swiss Ephemeris
  • mkdocs-material for docs site generation

docs.title: Chart dataclass
docs.text:
Drawing of SVG natal chart.

Source code in natal/chart.py 
@dataclass
class Chart:
    """Drawing of SVG natal chart."""

    natal_data: NatalData
    width: int = 1000
    height: int = 1000

    def svg(
        height: int,
        width: int,
        viewbox: str | None = None,
        version: str = "1.1",
        xmlns: str = "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg",
    ) -> svg_tag:
        """
        Generate an SVG element with sensible defaults.

        Parameters:
            height (int): The height of the SVG element.
            width (int): The width of the SVG element.
            viewbox (str, optional): The viewbox of the SVG element. Defaults to None.
            version (str, optional): The version of SVG. Defaults to "1.1".
            xmlns (str, optional): The XML namespace of SVG. Defaults to "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg".

        Returns:
            svg_tag: The generated SVG element.

        Example:
            >>> svg(100, 100).render()
            '<svg height="100" version="1.1" width="100" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"></svg>'

        """
        return svg_element(**locals())

    def sector(
        center_x: int,
        center_y: int,
        radius: int,
        start_deg: float,
        end_deg: float,
        fill: str = "white",
        stroke: str = "black",
        stroke_width: float = 1,
    ) -> svg_tag:
        """
        Creates a sector shape in SVG format.

        Args:
            center_x (int): The x-coordinate of the center of the sector.
            center_y (int): The y-coordinate of the center of the sector.
            radius (int): The radius of the sector.
            start_deg (float): The starting angle of the sector in degrees.
            end_deg (float): The ending angle of the sector in degrees.
            fill (str, optional): The fill color of the sector. Defaults to "white".
            stroke (str, optional): The stroke color of the sector. Defaults to "black".
            stroke_width (float, optional): The stroke width of the sector. Defaults to 1.

        Returns:
            svg_tag: The SVG tag representing the sector shape.

        Example:
            >>> sector(100, 100, 50, 0, 90).render()
            '<path d="M100 100 L50.0 100.0 A50 50 0 0 0 100.0 150.0 Z" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="1"></path>'
        """
        start_rad = radians(start_deg)
        end_rad = radians(end_deg)
        start_x = center_x - radius * cos(start_rad)
        start_y = center_y + radius * sin(start_rad)
        end_x = center_x - radius * cos(end_rad)
        end_y = center_y + radius * sin(end_rad)

        start_x, start_y, end_x, end_y = [
            round(val, 2) for val in (start_x, start_y, end_x, end_y)
        ]

        path_data = " ".join(
            (
                "M{} {}".format(center_x, center_y),
                "L{} {}".format(start_x, start_y),
                "A{} {} 0 0 0 {} {}".format(radius, radius, end_x, end_y),
                "Z",
            )
        )
        return path(d=path_data, fill=fill, stroke=stroke, stroke_width=stroke_width)

docs.title: sector(center_x: int, center_y: int, radius: int, start_deg: float, end_deg: float, fill: str = 'white', stroke: str = 'black', stroke_width: float = 1) -> svg_tag
docs.text:
Creates a sector shape in SVG format.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default center_x int

The x-coordinate of the center of the sector.

required center_y int

The y-coordinate of the center of the sector.

required radius int

The radius of the sector.

required start_deg float

The starting angle of the sector in degrees.

required end_deg float

The ending angle of the sector in degrees.

required fill str

The fill color of the sector. Defaults to "white".

'white' stroke str

Name Type Description svg_tag svg_tag

Example

sector(100, 100, 50, 0, 90).render() ''

Source code in natal/chart.py 
def sector(
    center_x: int,
    center_y: int,
    radius: int,
    start_deg: float,
    end_deg: float,
    fill: str = "white",
    stroke: str = "black",
    stroke_width: float = 1,
) -> svg_tag:
    """
    Creates a sector shape in SVG format.

    Args:
        center_x (int): The x-coordinate of the center of the sector.
        center_y (int): The y-coordinate of the center of the sector.
        radius (int): The radius of the sector.
        start_deg (float): The starting angle of the sector in degrees.
        end_deg (float): The ending angle of the sector in degrees.
        fill (str, optional): The fill color of the sector. Defaults to "white".
        stroke (str, optional): The stroke color of the sector. Defaults to "black".
        stroke_width (float, optional): The stroke width of the sector. Defaults to 1.

    Returns:
        svg_tag: The SVG tag representing the sector shape.

    Example:
        >>> sector(100, 100, 50, 0, 90).render()
        '<path d="M100 100 L50.0 100.0 A50 50 0 0 0 100.0 150.0 Z" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="1"></path>'
    """
    start_rad = radians(start_deg)
    end_rad = radians(end_deg)
    start_x = center_x - radius * cos(start_rad)
    start_y = center_y + radius * sin(start_rad)
    end_x = center_x - radius * cos(end_rad)
    end_y = center_y + radius * sin(end_rad)

    start_x, start_y, end_x, end_y = [
        round(val, 2) for val in (start_x, start_y, end_x, end_y)
    ]

    path_data = " ".join(
        (
            "M{} {}".format(center_x, center_y),
            "L{} {}".format(start_x, start_y),
            "A{} {} 0 0 0 {} {}".format(radius, radius, end_x, end_y),
            "Z",
        )
    )
    return path(d=path_data, fill=fill, stroke=stroke, stroke_width=stroke_width)

def svg(
    height: int,
    width: int,
    viewbox: str | None = None,
    version: str = "1.1",
    xmlns: str = "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg",
) -> svg_tag:
    """
    Generate an SVG element with sensible defaults.

    Parameters:
        height (int): The height of the SVG element.
        width (int): The width of the SVG element.
        viewbox (str, optional): The viewbox of the SVG element. Defaults to None.
        version (str, optional): The version of SVG. Defaults to "1.1".
        xmlns (str, optional): The XML namespace of SVG. Defaults to "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg".

    Returns:
        svg_tag: The generated SVG element.

    Example:
        >>> svg(100, 100).render()
        '<svg height="100" version="1.1" width="100" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"></svg>'

    """
    return svg_element(**locals())

docs.title: Planet
docs.text:
Bases: IntEnum

Source code in natal/enums.py 
class Planet(IntEnum):
    sun = 0
    moon = auto()
    mercury = auto()
    venus = auto()
    mars = auto()
    jupiter = auto()
    saturn = auto()
    uranus = auto()
    neptune = auto()
    pluto = auto()

    @property
    def symbol(self) -> str:
        return "☉☽☿♀♂♃♄♅♆♇"[self]

    @property
    def swe(self) -> str:
        """swiss ephemeris constant"""
        return "SE_" + self.name.upper()

    @property
    def ruling(self) -> list[Sign]:
        return (
            [Sign.aries],
            [Sign.cancer],
            [Sign.gemini, Sign.virgo],
            [Sign.taurus, Sign.libra],
            [Sign.aries],
            [Sign.sagittarius],
            [Sign.capricorn],
            [Sign.aquarius],
            [Sign.pisces],
            [Sign.scorpio],
        )[self]

docs.title: Sign
docs.text:
Bases: IntEnum

12 zodiac signs represented as 1-indexed IntEnum

Examples:

>>> Sign.aries
<Sign.aries: 1>
  • access by value
>>> Sign(1)
<Sign.aries: 1>
  • act as int
>>> "🍏🍊🍌"[Sign(1)]  
'🍊'
  • member properties
>>> Sign.taurus.symbol
'♉'

>>> Sign.gemini.modality
<Modality.mutable: 3>

>>> Sign.cancer.element
<Element.water: 4>

>>> Sign.leo.polarity
<Polarity.positive: 0>

>>> Sign.virgo.ruler
<Planet.mercury: 2>
Source code in natal/enums.py 
class Sign(IntEnum):
    """12 zodiac signs represented as 1-indexed IntEnum

    Examples:
        >>> Sign.aries
        <Sign.aries: 1>

        - access by value
        >>> Sign(1)
        <Sign.aries: 1>

        - act as int
        >>> "🍏🍊🍌"[Sign(1)]  
        '🍊'

        - member properties
        >>> Sign.taurus.symbol
        '♉'

        >>> Sign.gemini.modality
        <Modality.mutable: 3>

        >>> Sign.cancer.element
        <Element.water: 4>

        >>> Sign.leo.polarity
        <Polarity.positive: 0>

        >>> Sign.virgo.ruler
        <Planet.mercury: 2>
    """

    aries = 1
    taurus = 2
    gemini = 3
    cancer = 4
    leo = 5
    virgo = 6
    libra = 7
    scorpio = 8
    sagittarius = 9
    capricorn = 10
    aquarius = 11
    pisces = 12

    @property
    def symbol(self):
        return "♈♉♊♋♌♍♎♏♐♑♒♓"[self - 1]

    @property
    def modality(self) -> "Modality":
        return (list(Modality) * 4)[self - 1]

    @property
    def element(self) -> "Element":
        return (list(Element) * 3)[self - 1]

    @property
    def polarity(self) -> "Polarity":
        return (list(Polarity) * 6)[self - 1]

    @property
    def ruler(self) -> "Planet":
        return [
            Planet.mars,
            Planet.venus,
            Planet.mercury,
            Planet.moon,
            Planet.sun,
            Planet.mercury,
            Planet.venus,
            Planet.mars,
            Planet.jupiter,
            Planet.saturn,
            Planet.uranus,
            Planet.neptune,
        ][self - 1]

docs.title: NatalData dataclass
docs.text:
Data object for a natal chart.

Attributes:

Name Type Description name str

The name of the person the chart is for.

yr int

The year of birth.

mo int

The month of birth.

day int

The day of birth.

hr int

The hour of birth.

min int

The minute of birth.

city str

The city of birth.

Example

Natal("Alice", 1990, 1, 1, 12, 0, "New York") Natal(name='Alice', yr=1990, mo=1, day=1, hr=12, min=0, city='New York

Source code in natal/natal_data.py 
@dataclass
class NatalData:
    """Data object for a natal chart.

    Attributes:
        name: The name of the person the chart is for.
        yr: The year of birth.
        mo: The month of birth.
        day: The day of birth.
        hr: The hour of birth.
        min: The minute of birth.
        city: The city of birth.

    Example:
        >>> Natal("Alice", 1990, 1, 1, 12, 0, "New York")
        Natal(name='Alice', yr=1990, mo=1, day=1, hr=12, min=0, city='New York

    """

    name: str
    yr: int
    mo: int
    day: int
    hr: int
    min: int
    city: str
